topic: Someone finds a time capsule they don't remember burying
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the rough edges of the metal box with a fingertip, the cold seeping instantly through her skin. It was tucked beneath the loose paving stones in the forgotten corner of the garden, something she’d only noticed when pruning the overgrown lilac bush. The inscription on the lid was faded, almost illegible, but the date—1978—was starkly clear. A knot tightened in her chest, a strange, hollow feeling blooming in her stomach. She lifted the heavy container, its contents smelling faintly of aged paper and something metallic, and carried it inside. Every shadow in the living room seemed to stretch and deepen as she looked at the sealed vessel, a prickling sensation running down her arms. She tried to set it on the mantelpiece, but her hand trembled too violently, and she let it drop onto the hardwood floor with a muffled thud.

[story 2]

Mark found the box half-buried in the damp earth behind the old oak tree, a relic he’d never seen before. The sheer incongruity of it—a pristine, sealed container from decades past—made the hairs on his neck stand up. He knelt, brushing away the soil with hesitant fingers, his breath catching in his throat. The lock was rusted shut, requiring a careful, agonizing effort to pry open. When the lid finally creaked upward, a rush of stale air hit him, carrying the scent of dried lavender and something indefinably ancient. Mark leaned closer, his eyes scanning the contents—faded photographs, brittle letters tied with fraying ribbon. A wave of profound unease washed over him; it felt as if he had stumbled upon a secret he wasn't meant to know. He backed away slowly, his gaze fixed on the box as if expecting it to spring to life.

[story 3]

When Clara unearthed the time capsule, the weight of it felt disproportionate to its size. She sat on the porch steps, the afternoon sun doing little to warm the chill that had settled deep within her. The object was undeniably old, a heavy, tarnished cylinder sealed with wax. As she examined the inscription, a rapid, shallow breathing started in her chest. She kept glancing around the yard, half-expecting someone to appear, or perhaps the very air to shift. Inside, she saw sketches of constellations she didn't recognize and a small, folded note written in looping, elegant script. A cold certainty settled over her: this object was not hers, and the mystery it presented made her feel acutely exposed, as if she were witnessing something she shouldn't have seen.
